section.title: Glorious Things of Zion
section.sectionRationale: A short song of the sons of Korah, this psalm celebrates Zion as the city God founded and loves, then astonishes by declaring that former enemy nations will be registered as citizens born in Zion, a vision of the church gathered from every people that is fulfilled in Christ and pictured in the heavenly Jerusalem.
section.thesis: God has founded Zion and loves her above every other dwelling, and He will one day register even Israel's ancient enemies as citizens born within her, a promise of worldwide inclusion fulfilled in the church, made citizens of the heavenly Jerusalem through Christ.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Zion's secure foundation and God's special love for her, verses 1-3; the astonishing registration of the nations as citizens of Zion, verses 4-6; and the joyful song of those who find every spring of joy in God, verse 7.

units.label: God's City on the Holy Mountains

verses.text: He has {{yasad|founded}} His city on the holy mountains.
The LORD loves the gates of Zion more than all the dwellings of Jacob.
Glorious things are ascribed to you, O city of God. Selah

units.label: This One Was Born in Zion

verses.text: I will mention Rahab and Babylon among those who know Me, along with Philistia, Tyre, and Cush, when I say, 'This one was {{yalad|born}} in Zion.'
And it will be said of Zion: 'This one and that one were born in her, and the Most High Himself will establish her.'
The LORD will record in the register of the peoples: 'This one was born in Zion.' Selah
Singers and pipers will proclaim, 'All my {{mayan|springs}} of joy are in You.'

yasad.gloss: To found, to establish securely. God Himself, not human builders, has founded Zion on the holy mountains, giving her an unshakeable foundation.

yalad.gloss: To bear, to give birth. Used here of former enemy nations being registered as though born in Zion, picturing a new spiritual birthright and citizenship granted by God's own decree.

mayan.gloss: A spring or fountain of water. The psalm's closing image pictures every source of joy and life as flowing from God alone, the true spring behind the city of God.

basic.context: This short song of the sons of Korah celebrates Zion, the city God founded and loves above every other place in Israel. It then makes an astonishing claim: nations that were once Israel's enemies, Egypt, Babylon, Philistia, Tyre, and Cush, will one day be registered by God Himself as citizens born in Zion.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm describes Zion's unique historical status as the place of God's presence, and it prescribes an abiding hope: God's plan always included the nations, a hope that becomes reality in the worldwide, multiethnic people gathered into Christ's church.
basic.originalLanguages: God has yasad, founded, His city, verse 1, and the psalm pictures foreign peoples as yalad, born, in Zion, verses 4-6, a spiritual birthright granted by divine decree. The closing verse rejoices that every mayan, spring or fountain, of joy is found in God alone, verse 7.
basic.typology: Zion, the city of God's presence, points forward to the heavenly Jerusalem, the true and lasting city of God to which believers from every nation have already come. The registration of foreign nations as native-born citizens anticipates Gentiles being made fellow citizens with the saints in Christ.
basic.christ: Christ builds His church out of every nation and tongue, fulfilling this psalm's vision that former strangers to God's covenant would be registered as citizens born in Zion. Through faith in Him, believers are no longer strangers and aliens but fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God.
basic.god: God is shown as the founder of Zion who loves this city above all others, and as the sovereign King who personally registers citizens from every nation, including former enemies, granting them full standing in His city by His own gracious decree.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 2:12-19 pictures Gentiles, once strangers to the covenants of promise, made fellow citizens with the saints. Galatians 4:26 calls the Jerusalem above our mother. Hebrews 12:22-24 describes believers as having already come to Mount Zion, the city of the living God.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's vision of Zion as the city God founded and loves, and of former enemy nations registered as her citizens, finds its fulfillment in Christ's church, gathered from every nation and already, even now, citizens of the heavenly Jerusalem.

context.literary: A brief song of the sons of Korah in three parts, moving from Zion's foundation and God's love for her, verses 1-3, to the registration of the nations, verses 4-6, to a closing note of joy, verse 7, marked by two occurrences of Selah.
context.historical: Sung in the Jerusalem temple, likely during festival worship when Israel gathered and could envision the surrounding nations, some of them former oppressors, one day turning to know and worship the LORD.
context.canonical: Part of the Korahite collection celebrating Zion within Book III, this psalm's vision of the nations gathered to God anticipates prophetic promises in Isaiah and Zechariah and is realized in the New Testament church.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To celebrate Zion as the city of God's special presence and love, and to declare God's astonishing plan to grant full citizenship there to peoples who were once Israel's enemies.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific vision of Zion's future glory; it prescribes an ongoing hope and mission, that God's people should expect and welcome the ingathering of the nations rather than guard their privileges jealously.

debates.question: Are the nations named in verse 4, Rahab, Babylon, Philistia, Tyre, and Cush, meant to represent literal future converts from those specific peoples, or all Gentile nations symbolically?

views.label: They represent specific, literal nations that would one day turn to the LORD
views.case: Each nation named had a real, documented history of hostility toward Israel, so their specific inclusion highlights the concrete reversal of real historical enmity.

views.label: They function as representative examples standing for all Gentile nations broadly
views.case: Together these five nations span every point of the compass around Israel, Egypt to the south, Babylon to the east, Philistia and Tyre to the west, Cush to the far south, functioning as a summary of the whole known world.

debates.bearing: Either way, the psalm announces that God's saving plan was never narrowly nationalistic, but always included the ingathering of the nations, fulfilled in the church drawn from every people.

study.originalLanguages: God yasad, founded, His city on the holy mountains, verse 1, and the psalm pictures foreign peoples as yalad, born, in Zion by divine decree, verses 4-6. The closing song declares that every mayan, spring of joy, is found in God alone, verse 7, the true and lasting fountain behind the city of God.

typology.type: Zion, the city God founded and loves, verses 1-2
typology.fulfillment: Points to the heavenly Jerusalem, the true city of the living God to which believers have already come, Hebrews 12:22.

typology.type: Former enemy nations registered as born in Zion, verses 4-6
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled as Gentiles are made fellow citizens with the saints and members of God's household through Christ, Ephesians 2:19.

study.christ: Christ gathers His church from every nation, fulfilling this psalm's vision that former strangers, even Israel's ancient enemies, would be registered as citizens born in Zion. Through union with Him, believers who were once far off are brought near and made fellow citizens with the saints, no longer strangers and aliens, Ephesians 2:13, 19.
study.god: God is the founder and lover of Zion, the sovereign King who personally keeps the register of the peoples, and the gracious Lord who delights to grant full citizenship in His city to those who were once His enemies, by His own free decree.

crossReferences.note: Gentiles, once strangers to the covenants, are made fellow citizens with the saints, fulfilling this psalm's vision of nations born in Zion.
The Jerusalem above is free and is the mother of all believers, echoing this psalm's exaltation of Zion.
Believers have already come to Mount Zion, the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem.

study.oneStory: This short song celebrates Zion, the city God founded and loves, and astonishingly promises citizenship there to Israel's former enemies. That promise comes true in Christ's church, gathered from every nation and already registered as citizens of the heavenly Jerusalem, the true city of God.

quiz.q: According to verse 1, who founded the city of Zion?

options.text: God Himself, on the holy mountains.
options.note: The psalm opens by attributing Zion's foundation directly to God, not to human builders.

options.text: King David alone, by his own military strength.
options.note: David did historically capture the stronghold of Zion, but this psalm credits the city's deeper, true foundation to God Himself, not merely to David's conquest.

options.text: The Philistines.
options.note: The Philistines are named among the nations later drawn to Zion, not as its founders.

options.text: No one; the city arose by chance.
options.note: The psalm is explicit that God founded the city with purpose.

quiz.q: What astonishing declaration is made about the nations in verses 4-6?

options.text: That nations once hostile to Israel, like Egypt and Babylon, will be registered as citizens born in Zion.
options.note: This is the psalm's central surprise, that former enemies would gain full citizenship by God's decree.

options.text: That these nations will be permanently destroyed.
options.note: The passage announces their inclusion as citizens, not their destruction.

options.text: That Israel will conquer and enslave these nations.
options.note: The vision is one of gracious inclusion, not conquest or enslavement.

options.text: That these nations, once hostile to Israel, will change their relationship to God.
options.note: This is true in a general sense, but the specific declaration in verses 4-6 goes further: they will be registered as citizens actually born in Zion.

quiz.q: What does verse 7 identify as the source of all joy?

options.text: God Himself; 'all my springs of joy are in You.'
options.note: The closing verse locates every source of joy in God alone.

options.text: The wealth of the city of Zion.
options.note: The psalm points beyond material wealth to God Himself as the source of joy.

options.text: Military victory over enemy nations.
options.note: The psalm's tone toward the nations is inclusion, not celebration of conquest.

options.text: The city of Zion itself.
options.note: Zion is closely connected to this joy, but verse 7 specifically locates the true source of joy in God Himself, not the city as such.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament fulfill this psalm's vision of nations registered as citizens of Zion?

options.text: Gentiles are made fellow citizens with the saints and members of God's household through Christ.
options.note: Ephesians 2:12-19 describes exactly this fulfillment, former strangers now full citizens through Christ.

options.text: It has no fulfillment in the New Testament.
options.note: Paul directly applies this theme of Gentile inclusion in Ephesians 2.

options.text: It is fulfilled only by the physical rebuilding of Jerusalem's walls.
options.note: The fulfillment is spiritual citizenship through Christ, not merely physical construction.

options.text: It is fulfilled as Gentile believers come to worship the true God through Christ.
options.note: This is true and closely related, though the fullest New Testament expression, Ephesians 2:12-19, speaks specifically of fellow citizenship, not merely general worship.

quiz.q: What does the register kept by the LORD in verse 6 signify?

options.text: God's own personal, authoritative record of who belongs to His city.
options.note: The verse pictures God Himself, not human officials, keeping this register of citizenship.

options.text: A census for taxation purposes only.
options.note: The register is about spiritual citizenship and belonging, not merely civic taxation.

options.text: A list of Israel's enemies to be destroyed.
options.note: The register lists those counted as belonging to Zion, not enemies marked for destruction.

options.text: A record affirming true spiritual citizenship and belonging.
options.note: This is close to correct, but slightly understates that the register is specifically God's own personal, authoritative decree, as verse 6 emphasizes.

questions: This psalm celebrates that God Himself founded and loves Zion. Where do you find your own sense of belonging and security, and does it rest on something as sure as God's own foundation?
Verses 4-6 declare that God would register former enemy nations as citizens of Zion. Is there a person or group you find hard to imagine welcomed fully into God's family?
The psalm pictures citizenship in Zion as granted by God's own decree, not earned. How does knowing your standing before God rests on His declaration, not your effort, change how you approach Him?
Verse 7 says all springs of joy are found in God. Where have you been seeking joy from a source other than God this week?
Believers today are citizens of the heavenly Jerusalem, Hebrews 12:22. How should that heavenly citizenship shape the way you view your earthly circumstances and loyalties?

translationNotes.note: Literally the Hebrew reads His foundation is on the holy mountains, a construction the BSB renders as He has founded His city for clarity.
Rahab is a poetic name for Egypt, and Cush refers to the upper Nile region, so the four named peoples together span the compass around Israel.
This one was born in Zion functions as a formula of legal registration, granting foreign individuals the same standing as native-born citizens.

meta.notes: BSB text obtained via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com's BSB (Berean Standard Bible), cross-checked against the section map bounds (Psalm 87:1-7, 7 verses). Verse 4's em dashes in the source were converted to commas per house style. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) was confirmed this session as a dedicated message on Psalm 87 itself, so status is none; a Piper message on Psalm 50 that discusses Psalm 87 at length was found but was not primarily on this passage.
